questions.question: List four common input devices used in computer graphics and give one primary application for each.
keyPoints: Keyboard – text entry, shortcuts, UI navigation
Graphics tablet – free‑hand drawing, illustration, pressure sensitivity
Scanner – converting printed images/photos to digital raster data

questions.question: Name three output devices used in computer graphics and briefly describe the underlying technology each uses to produce visual output.
keyPoints: CRT monitor – electron gun fires electrons at phosphor‑coated screen, creating images
LCD/LED monitor – liquid crystal layer modulates light from a backlight; LED backlight improves efficiency
Inkjet printer – sprays microscopic droplets of ink onto paper to form text/graphics
Laser printer – uses toner fused by heat after electrostatic image creation on a photoconductor

questions.question: Explain the operation of a flatbed scanner and a digital camera as input devices in computer graphics. Discuss key specifications such as optical resolution (DPI), sensor type (CCD vs CMOS), colour depth, dynamic range, and common interfaces.
keyPoints: Flatbed scanner – moving scan head with CCD or CIS sensor captures line‑by‑line image; optical resolution (e.g., 300‑2400 DPI), colour depth (24‑48 bit), dynamic range
Digital camera – lens projects scene onto CCD/CMOS sensor; effective megapixels, colour filter array (Bayer), colour depth (12‑14 bit), dynamic range
Key specifications: optical resolution/DPI, sensor type, colour depth, dynamic range, interface (USB, FireWire, SDI)
Impact on graphics work: higher DPI enables finer detail, larger colour depth improves gradient fidelity, adequate dynamic range reduces blown highlights/shadows

questions.question: Discuss the evolution of display technologies in computer graphics from CRT to modern OLED and MicroLED. Include at least five milestones, the underlying working principles, and key performance metrics such as resolution, refresh rate, contrast ratio, colour gamut, and response time.
keyPoints: Milestones: CRT → CRT with shadow mask → LCD → LED‑backlit LCD → OLED → MicroLED
Working principles: electron beam & phosphors (CRT); liquid crystal + backlight (LCD/LED); organic emissive layers (OLED); inorganic micro‑LED arrays (MicroLED)
Critical metrics: resolution (HD/4K/8K), refresh rate (60‑144 Hz), contrast ratio (1,000:1‑1,000,000:1), colour gamut (sRGB, DCI‑P3, Rec. 2020), response time (1‑5 ms)
Design considerations for graphics professionals: colour accuracy, HDR support, power consumption, viewing angles, and form‑factor flexibility
